g.data-narration: Welcome to Decision Tree Induction Algorithms. We'll explore how machines automatically build decision trees from data.
A Decision Tree is a supervised learning algorithm that models decisions as a tree structure with nodes and branches.
g.text: A supervised learning algorithm that models
decisions as a tree structure for classification

g.data-narration: Building decision trees manually is impossible with many features. We need algorithms to automatically select the best splits.
g.text: Why Do We Need Tree Induction Algorithms?
Manual tree building is infeasible for large datasets. Algorithms automatically:
1. Select which feature to split on

g.data-narration: Here's how a decision tree looks. The root node splits the data, internal nodes make decisions, and leaf nodes give predictions.
Entropy measures the impurity in a dataset. Pure datasets have zero entropy. Mixed datasets have higher entropy values.
g.text: Where pᵢ = proportion of class i in S

g.data-narration: Information Gain measures the reduction in entropy after splitting. Higher IG means a better split. The algorithm picks the attribute with highest IG.
g.text: Measures reduction in entropy after splitting on attribute A

g.data-narration: Gini Impurity is used by CART algorithm. It's faster to compute than entropy and measures the probability of misclassification.
g.text: Probability of incorrectly classifying random element

g.data-narration: Gain Ratio corrects Information Gain's bias toward features with many values. It's used by C4.5 algorithm.
g.text: SplitInfo penalizes attributes with many unique values

g.data-narration: For regression trees, we use variance reduction instead of entropy. This minimizes prediction variance in subsets.
g.text: Measures spread of continuous target values

g.data-narration: Stopping criteria prevent overfitting by halting tree growth. Common criteria include maximum depth and minimum samples.
g.text: Stopping Criteria - When to Stop Splitting

g.data-narration: Pruning reduces tree complexity to prevent overfitting. Pre-pruning stops early, while post-pruning removes branches afterward.
g.text: Stops tree growth early using stopping criteria
Builds full tree, then removes weak branches

g.data-narration: Let's compare the three major algorithms: ID3, C4.5, and CART. Each has different strengths and use cases.
g.text: Algorithm Comparison: ID3 vs C4.5 vs CART

g.data-narration: Let's work through an example. We calculate entropy first, then information gain for each feature to choose the best split.
g.text: Weather becomes Root (highest IG) → Sunny, Overcast, Rainy branches

g.data-narration: Decision trees have important pros and cons. They're interpretable but can overfit. Let's summarize them.
That's it! Remember: ID3 uses Information Gain, C4.5 uses Gain Ratio, CART uses Gini. Use pruning to prevent overfitting.
g.text: • ID3: Information Gain | Categorical only | No pruning
• C4.5: Gain Ratio | Handles continuous | Post-pruning
• CART: Gini/Variance | Binary trees | Cost-complexity pruning
